title: Probability & Counting
blurb: Probability rules, Venn diagrams, tree diagrams, contingency tables, the fundamental counting principle and arrangements with restrictions.

questions.context: For two events $A$ and $B$ it is given that $P(A)=0{,}35$, $P(B)=0{,}5$ and $P(A\text{ or }B)=0{,}675$.

parts.q: Determine $P(A\text{ and }B)$.
Are the events $A$ and $B$ independent? Motivate your answer with a calculation.
solution: Since $P(A)\times P(B)=P(A\text{ and }B)=0{,}175$, the events ARE independent

parts.q: Determine the probability that neither $A$ nor $B$ occurs.

questions.context: In a group of $120$ Grade 12 learners at a school in Tshwane, $65$ take Mathematics (M), $50$ take Physical Sciences (P) and $25$ take both subjects. The rest take neither subject.

parts.q: Draw a Venn diagram to represent this information, showing the number of learners in each region.
parts.answer: Regions: only M $=40$, both $=25$, only P $=25$, neither $=30$

parts.q: Determine the probability that a randomly chosen learner takes neither of the two subjects.
Determine $P(M\text{ or }P)$.
Are the events M and P mutually exclusive? Give a reason for your answer.
solution: Since learners CAN take both subjects, the events are NOT mutually exclusive

questions.context: Lerato travels to school either by taxi or on foot. The probability that she takes a taxi is $0{,}6$. If she takes a taxi, the probability that she is late is $0{,}1$. If she walks, the probability that she is late is $0{,}3$.

parts.q: Draw a tree diagram to represent this situation. Show the probability on each branch.
solution: From Taxi: Late ($0{,}1$) and Not late ($0{,}9$)
From Walk: Late ($0{,}3$) and Not late ($0{,}7$)

parts.q: Calculate the probability that Lerato is late for school on a given day.
Calculate the probability that Lerato is NOT late for school.

questions.context: A spaza shop owner in Soweto recorded how $150$ customers paid and whether they bought airtime. Of the $90$ customers who paid cash, $45$ bought airtime. Of the $60$ customers who paid by card, $25$ bought airtime. (Contingency table totals: cash $=90$, card $=60$; airtime $=70$, no airtime $=80$.)

parts.q: Determine the probability that a randomly selected customer paid cash.
Determine the probability that a randomly selected customer paid cash AND bought airtime.
Are the events 'paid cash' and 'bought airtime' independent? Motivate your answer with calculations.
solution: $0{,}28\ne 0{,}3$, so the events are NOT independent

parts.q: Determine the probability that a randomly selected customer paid cash OR bought airtime.

questions.context: Consider the letters of the word MOSSEL (as in Mossel Bay). Note that the letter S appears twice.

parts.q: In how many different ways can all six letters be arranged in a row?
In how many of these arrangements does the word begin AND end with the letter S?
solution: Place the two S's in the first and last positions (identical letters: 1 way)
Arrange the remaining 4 different letters in the middle: $4!=24$

parts.q: If one of the $360$ arrangements is chosen at random, determine the probability that it begins and ends with the letter S.

A security gate code consists of $3$ different letters of the alphabet followed by $2$ digits (digits MAY repeat). Using the fundamental counting principle, determine how many different codes are possible.

questions.context:
In a group of $70$ learners, each learner belongs to at least one of three clubs: Debate ($D$), Chess ($C$) and Art ($A$). The following is known:
- $15$ belong to Debate only, $12$ to Chess only, $10$ to Art only
- $8$ belong to Debate and Chess only
- $6$ belong to Debate and Art only
- $5$ belong to Chess and Art only
- $x$ belong to all three clubs

parts.q: Calculate the value of $x$.
solution: All regions add up to the total of $70$

parts.q: Determine the probability that a randomly chosen learner belongs to at least TWO clubs.
Determine the probability that a learner belongs to exactly ONE club.
Determine the probability that a learner belongs to the Debate club.

questions.context: On any given day in winter, the probability of load-shedding in a town is $0{,}4$. If there is load-shedding, the probability that Thabo is late for work is $0{,}7$. If there is no load-shedding, the probability that he is late is $0{,}15$.

parts.q: Represent the given information on a tree diagram, showing the probability on each branch.
solution: From No load-shedding: Late $0{,}15$ / Not late $0{,}85$

parts.q: Calculate the probability that there is load-shedding AND Thabo is late.
Calculate the probability that Thabo is late for work on a given day.

questions.context: A code consists of $5$ characters in the form: letter, letter, digit, digit, digit. There are $26$ letters and the digits are $0$ to $9$.

parts.q: How many different codes can be formed if letters and digits may be repeated?
How many different codes can be formed if: the two letters may NOT be repeated, the code may NOT start with A or B, and the last digit must be even?
solution: First letter: not A or B $\Rightarrow 24$ choices
Second letter: any letter except the first $\Rightarrow 25$ choices

parts.q: The restriction that the code may not start with A or B is now removed (all other conditions in the previous question stay). Calculate the percentage increase in the number of possible codes.
solution: New total: $26	imes 25	imes 10	imes 10	imes 5=325\,000$

questions.context: Eight learners of different heights stand in a random straight line, one behind the other.

parts.q: In how many different ways can the eight learners be arranged in the line?
Calculate the probability that the two tallest learners stand next to each other.
solution: Treat the two tallest as one unit: $7!$ arrangements, $	imes 2$ for their order $=7!	imes 2=10\,080$

parts.q: Calculate the probability that exactly THREE learners stand between the two tallest learners.
solution: The pair must occupy positions differing by $4$: $(1;5),(2;6),(3;7),(4;8)$ — $4$ position pairs
$	imes 2$ for the order of the two tallest, $	imes 6!$ for the other six learners

questions.context: Consider the letters of the word NUMBER. All six letters are different.

parts.q: How many different arrangements of all the letters are possible?
Calculate the probability that an arrangement starts and ends with a vowel.
solution: Vowels are U and E ($2$ vowels)
First position: $2$ vowel choices; last position: $1$ remaining vowel; middle $4$ letters: $4!=24$

parts.q: Calculate the probability that the letters M and B are next to each other.
solution: Treat M and B as one unit: $5!	imes 2=240$
